verses.text: 8:1-36 Em Êx 28–29, o Senhor deu a Moisés instruções específicas para o vestuário e para a consagração de Arão e seus filhos como sacerdotes. Em Êx 39.1-31; 40.12-16; e Lv 8, as ordens foram cumpridas. Moisés não apenas passou as instruções verbalmente; encenou os rituais para prover um exemplo para os sacerdotes até o último detalhe, exatamente como o Senhor lhe havia ordenado (Lv 8.9,13,17,21,29). Como a penalidade pela desobediência era a morte, Deus garantiu que os sacerdotes soubessem exatamente o que fazer.

8:2 Estes itens também são mencionados em Êx 29.1-3,7. • vestes sagradas: Cf. Êx 28. • O óleo da unção consistia em ingredientes especiais para dar-lhe um aroma único. Estes incluíam mirra, canela, cana e cássia (flores secas da árvore de canela) misturadas em azeite de oliva (cf. Êx 30.22-33). • novilho para a oferta pelo pecado: Cf. Lv 4.3-12. • O primeiro dos dois carneiros devia ser um holocausto integral (Lv 8.18), enquanto o segundo era um sacrifício especial de consagração (8.22). • cesta de pão feito sem fermento: Cf. Lv 6.19-23.

8:3 gather thou all the congregation together, &c.—It was manifestly expedient for the Israelitish people to be satisfied that Aaron's appointment to the high dignity of the priesthood was not a personal intrusion, nor a family arrangement between him and Moses; and nothing, therefore, could be a more prudent or necessary measure, for impressing a profound conviction of the divine origin and authority of the priestly institution, than to summon a general assembly of the people, and in their presence perform the solemn ceremonies of inauguration, which had been prescribed by divine authority.
8:4 O texto repetidamente enfatiza que Moisés seguiu as instruções do Senhor, obedecendo aos mandamentos dados em Êx 29 (cf. também Lv 8.9,13,17,21,29,36).

8:5 And Moses said unto the congregation,.... Having convened them, he opened to them the reason of their being called together, which was not done of himself, but by divine direction: this is the thing which the Lord commanded to be done; namely, what follows, concerning the consecration of Aaron and his sons to be priests, and the investiture of them with that office, attended with various rites and ceremonies to be performed, of which they were to be witnesses.

8:6 Ser lavado . . . com água era o primeiro passo para a purificação da impureza. Os sacerdotes tinham de estar cerimonialmente puros antes de poderem servir ao Senhor em santidade (Êx 29.4; cf. Sl 24.4).

8:7 A túnica (cf. Êx 28.39) era tecida de tecido de linho com padrões. • O mesmo tipo de material usado para as cortinas do Tabernáculo era usado para o éfode bordado (cf. Êx 28.4,6-14). A forma e tamanho precisos desta peça não são claros, mas parece ter sido um colete longo sem mangas, ajustado ao corpo e amarrado na cintura. Era unido nos ombros por montagens de ouro; cada montagem trazia uma pedra de ônix gravada com os nomes de seis das tribos de Israel (Êx 28.9). Mais tarde, um éfode também se referia a uma imagem venerada (Jz 8.27; 18.17). • O cinto decorativo era bordado e feito dos mesmos materiais que o éfode (cf. Êx 28.8).

8:8 O peitoral (cf. Êx 28.15-30) era feito de linho bordado dobrado duplo. Trazia doze pedras, cada uma gravada com o nome de uma tribo de Israel. Era suspenso sobre o coração por uma corrente de ouro presa às ombreiras de ouro do éfode. • O Urim e o Tumim eram colocados no bolso do peitoral (Êx 28.30; 1Sm 23.9-12; 30.7-8); a função precisa desses itens é incerta. Geralmente são pensados como duas pedras, talvez de cores diferentes, que eram lançadas como sortes. Aparentemente podiam dar uma resposta positiva, neutra ou negativa a perguntas que não podiam ser resolvidas de outra forma (Êx 28.30; Nm 27.21; 1Sm 28.6). Sua menção na literatura profética do AT escrita séculos depois (Ed 2.63; Ne 7.65) demonstra como esses itens eram importantes para o ofício do sacerdote.

8:9 O turbante usado pelo sumo sacerdote era feito de linho e era distinto dos cocares dos sacerdotes comuns (Êx 28.40). A medalha de ouro era suspensa do turbante e trazia uma inscrição que dizia “Santo ao Senhor” (Êx 28.36). Com os nomes das tribos em seus ombros e sobre seu coração, e a dedicação ao Senhor em sua testa, o sumo sacerdote era claramente marcado como representante do povo diante de Deus e representante de Deus diante do povo. Neste papel mediador, o sumo sacerdote modelava o padrão para todos os sacerdotes.

8:10 tornando-os santos: Deus é o Santo (Sl 22.3; 78.41). No sentido mais estrito, apenas Deus pode tornar uma pessoa ou coisa santa (Lv 20.8; 21.15,23; 22.9,16,32; Êx 31.13; cf. Hb 2.11); as ações de Moisés simplesmente reconheciam e declaravam o que Deus havia feito (também em Lv 8.11,12,15).

8:11 And he sprinkled thereof upon the altar seven times,.... The altar of burnt offering; the order for anointing it is given in Exo 30:28 but in that no directions are given for the manner of doing it by sprinkling, nor the number of times it was to be sprinkled: hence Jarchi confesses his ignorance, and says,"I know not how it was ordered about these sprinklings;''but no doubt Moses was instructed of God in what manner to anoint it, and how often;

8:12 And he poured of the anointing oil upon Aaron's head,.... Which ran down to his beard, and to the collar of his coat, the robe of the ephod, but not to the skirts of his garments, as we wrongly render it, Ps 133:2. Jarchi says it was first poured on his head, and after that he put it between his eyebrows, and drew it with his finger here and there, or from one eyebrow to another: Maimonides (w) gives a like account, with some addition;
8:13 Para o vestuário dos sacerdotes comuns, cf. Êx 28.40-41.

8:14 sacrifício pelo pecado (cf. Lv 4.3-12): Duas ofertas expiatórias eram feitas — o sacrifício pelo pecado (Lv 8.14-17) e o holocausto integral (8.18-21). • Pondo suas mãos sobre a cabeça do novilho (cf. Lv 1.4), Arão e seus filhos se identificavam com o sacrifício (também em 8.18,22).

8:15 Normalmente, o sumo sacerdote desempenhava esses deveres sacerdotais. Contudo, não seria apropriado para os sacerdotes consagrarem a si mesmos, então Moisés, agindo como representante de Deus, instituiu o sacerdócio.

8:16 And he took all the fat that was upon the inwards,.... Called the "omentum": and the caul above the liver; the lobe upon the liver, as the Septuagint; or "the caul" and "the liver", so says Jarchi; the liver separately, for he took a little of the liver with it, the caul: and the two kidneys, and their fat, and Moses burned it upon the altar: the fat of these several parts, which has been often observed was done; and in imitation of which, the same has been done by the Persians and their Magi, as related by Strabo (x) and others (y);

8:17 But the bullock and his hide, his flesh, and his dung, he burnt with fire without the camp,.... Aben Ezra observes, that some say that he did this himself; and others, that it was done by orders, that is, he ordered others to do it, which seems probable enough: as the Lord commanded Moses; Exo 29:14.
8:18 carneiro para o holocausto: Cf. nota em Lv 1.10-13.

8:19 And he killed it,.... That is, Moses killed the ram, as the Septuagint version expresses it: and Moses sprinkled the blood upon the altar round about; as he did the blood of the bullock, Lev 8:15.
8:20 And he cut the ram into pieces,.... Cut off its head and quartered it: and Moses burnt the head, and the pieces, and the fat; even all of it, as the following verse shows.

8:21 And he washed the inwards and the legs in water,.... The one being taken out, the other cut off: and Moses burnt the whole ram upon the altar: it was a burnt sacrifice for a sweet savour, and an offering made by fire unto the Lord; as the Lord commanded Moses; see Exo 29:18.
8:22 carneiro de consagração: Cf. Êx 29.19-34.

8:23 O lóbulo da orelha, o polegar e o dedão do pé de Arão representavam toda a sua pessoa. Este ato enfatizava o papel do ofício sacerdotal, com o sangue ressaltando os laços ao sistema sacrificial. A função básica do sacerdote era a mediação entre Deus e seu povo. Para fazer isso eficazmente, ele precisava de ouvidos que pudessem ouvir as palavras de Deus e as necessidades do seu povo, mãos prontas para fazer a vontade de Deus e pés prontos para levá-lo onde fosse necessário.

8:24 Moses put of the blood upon the tip of their right ear, and upon the thumbs of their right hands, and upon the great toes of their right feet: The triple-point anointing of Aaron's sons consecrates ear (to hear God's word), hand (to perform priestly service), and foot (to walk in holy paths). The whole bodily life is set apart at three deliberate points (cp. Lev 14:14, 17 for parallel cleansing of a healed leper).

8:25 He took the fat, and the rump, and all the fat that was upon the inwards: The ram of consecration's inner fats — given wholly to the Lord. The detail repeats Leviticus's central pattern: the priest, like the sin offering, surrenders the hidden inner substance to God. Consecration is from the inside out.

8:26 And out of the basket of unleavened bread,.... Moses was ordered to take, Lev 8:2, that was before the Lord; being brought to the tabernacle, where now the Lord had taken up his residence: he took one unleavened cake; which had no oil in it or upon it: and a cake of oiled bread; which was mixed and tempered with oil: and one wafer; which was anointed with oil: and put them upon the fat, and upon the right shoulder;

8:27 And he put all upon Aaron's hands, and upon his son's hands,.... The fat and the right shoulder, with the cakes upon them: and waved them for a wave offering before the Lord; See Gill on Exo 29:24.

8:28 And Moses took them from off their hands,.... After they had been waved before the Lord: and burnt them upon the altar, upon the burnt offering; of the other ram; or after that burnt offering, as Jarchi, who observes, that we do not find that the shoulder of peace offerings was offered in any place but this, it belonged to the priest; but this being at the consecration of the priests, it was offered to the Lord by Moses, to whom it seems to have belonged, as the breast also, but that was not burnt, but eaten: and the same writer affirms, that Moses ministered all the seven days of the consecration in a white shirt, or surplice;
8:29 Moisés funcionou como sacerdote na cerimônia de consagração e por isso recebeu a porção do sacerdote da oferta (cf. Lv 8.15).
8:30 óleo da unção: Cf. nota em Lv 8.2; cf. também Sl 133.2.

8:31 A oferta de consagração era uma variação da oferta pacífica (Êx 29.28). Era uma refeição (Lv 7.11-18,28-34) compartilhada entre Deus (as partes queimadas no altar) e os sacerdotes. A ordem de cozinhar . . . e comer a oferta de consagração aparece primeiro em Êx 29.32.

8:32 And that which remaineth of the flesh and of the bread,.... Until the next morning, which could not be eaten by Aaron and his sons: shall ye burn with fire; that it might not be corrupted, nor put to common nor superstitious uses.

8:33 ye shall not go out of the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, &c.—After all these preliminaries, they had still to undergo a week's probation in the court of the tabernacle before they obtained permission to enter into the interior of the sacred building. During the whole of that period the same sacrificial rites were observed as on the first day, and they were expressly admonished that the smallest breach of any of the appointed observances would lead to the certain forfeiture of their lives [Lev 8:35].

8:34 ordenado pelo Senhor: Os detalhes dos rituais de sacrifício eram simbólicos e importantes por si mesmos. Contudo, o mais importante, os sacerdotes deviam obedecer aos mandamentos de Deus. A obediência completa a Deus é um tema importante tanto no AT quanto no NT: “Obedecer é melhor que sacrificar” (1Sm 15.22).

8:35 sete dias: Esta ordem enfatizava a santidade absoluta de Deus e exigia conformidade estrita com seus mandamentos. Os sacerdotes, agora ungidos como servos santos de Deus, deviam permanecer no pátio do Tabernáculo por sete dias antes de aventurar-se à vida cotidiana.

8:36 So Aaron and his sons did - This chapter shows the exact fulfillment of the commands delivered to Moses, Exodus 29; and consequently the complete preparation of Aaron and his sons to fill the awfully important office of priests and mediators between God and Israel, to offer sacrifices and make atonement for the sins of the people. "Thus," says Mr. Ainsworth, "the covenant of the priesthood was confirmed unto the tribe of Levi in Aaron and his sons, which covenant was life and peace, Mal 2:5.
